But see, a whole lot of people can connect with that. And so you got real strong points of connection there. Either the disapproving relatives, or the desire to prove oneself to somebody. See, that's good material. Along the issue of the story, we talked about J. Peter Menon, how he can take a regular jacket and turn it into a $500 jacket. In an info marketing world, I think of it as romancing the product. In this case, do I romance the picture of them that they could become if they joined my program? Would that be the way to... It's hard to romance the CD, right? Right. So, yeah, it's about, I mean, look, they either join these things for one of several reasons, basically. So they join it out of pain, desperation, frustration, you're the life raft, they're going down for the third time, hope against hope, these guys can save me. Or they join in some cases because of fraternal influence. Oh, two of my buddies are in this thing, so I want to be in this thing with them. But mostly they join for aspirational reasons. They join in search of the, I don't mean monetary value, I mean the emotional value that they want, whether that be validation or whether that be a change in the process that they go through every day in order to make their money. But they join for those aspirational reasons. And so that's exactly what you have to continually beat up and seductively present. I mean, they want to match themselves up to a picture that is appealing to them. And ideally it's a movie, it's a moving picture, not a static photograph. And you have to make some generalizations, but different ones are different for different people. But that's exactly what you're selling. I mean, nobody buys, nobody buys three days in a room. And as a matter of fact, nobody wants three days in a room. You don't want it, I don't want it. Nobody buys three meetings a year. And if they do, they won't pay much for it because it's commoditized. And you know, Peterman's a good example. I mean, if you like going to dog food categories. So at the commodity level, we go over to the grocery store and there's dog food on the shelf and it's whatever the hell it is. And you know, it doesn't take a story to sell it. The dog needs food, go get dog food, right? But there's also really expensive dog food. And almost all the really expensive dog food is being sold with an incredible story. In some cases, it's the origin and mission story of the dog lover who is making sure that it's only organic ingredients. And in some cases, it's the medical science story. There's dog food being sold that extends their life. There's dog food that makes them smarter. There's, you know, on and on and on and on and on. So they're selling aspirations. So I want my dog to look better, have a shinier coat. You know, I want my dog to be the smartest dog in the neighborhood. I want, you know, and they're selling dog food for unconscionable prices. But, you know, there we are. So, but our commodity is not the books and the tapes and our commodity is actually, you know, all the deliverables, including meetings or coaching or calls or whatever. And they don't, the commodities themselves create very low price point and very low appeal. But the aspirations that they lead to have very high value. The reason that we, I brought this up is because in our first sales letter, we had a lot of people calling asking, so what exactly do I get? And we're saying, okay, well that's a problem because if they're confused, that's a bad prospect. So we went back and we revamped the sales letter to say, here's what you get, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. Now I'm thinking, well that's all product. That's all CD, tele-seminar, website. That's not what they want to buy. So now do I take that one step further and say, here's what you get, but here's what you really get. You get the lifestyle. Yeah, that's really, I mean, it may not be quite that clumsy and clunky, but that's exactly the deal. I mean, you have to explain what they get because they have to justify the, you know, and bulk counts for something. Like there's certain realities in the seminar business, for example, you can't get a big amount of money for a one day seminar if people have to travel to get there because it doesn't make any sense to them. You can't get them to travel to a one day seminar cheap if the price is lower than the airfare to get there. That doesn't make any sense to them. And it doesn't make any difference what aspiration could be achieved that deliverable itself is just unacceptable, you know. In theft control, just to prove we could do it, we had a single audio cassette tape we sold for $500. But those circumstances are extraordinarily rare, you know. So you do have to tell them what they get, but in reality, like if you take our dentistry for diabetics pitch be an area exclusive, right, so there's a whole list of shit they get done for them. And that's the part that they get it done for them is appealing. But what's more important is now the money argument of you spend this and the patient comes in and you're, you know. But what they're really buying is other stuff. They're buying the status of being the only person in the area who's part of this unique thing. They're buying the idea of ease that now a lot of patients are gonna show up who are easier to deal with and more compliant and more respectful and more appreciative. So they're buying aspirational things, right. And so you gotta cover the other base, but really what they're gonna buy is the aspiration. And the best way to present the aspirations is stories.
